goal: Become a proficient DevOps Engineer with strong skills in Linux, Docker, Kubernetes, and CI/CD.

phases.title: Phase 1: Linux & Git Foundations for DevOps
phases.description: This phase solidifies essential Linux command-line skills and advanced Git practices crucial for any DevOps role, ensuring a strong base for subsequent topics.

days.title: Advanced Linux Command-Line & Filesystem
days.explanation: Mastering the Linux command line is fundamental for managing servers, automating tasks, and interacting with containerized environments. Understanding filesystem navigation and manipulation is key to efficient operations.

commands.desc: Finds all '.log' files and lists them with long format
Lists all processes and filters for 'nginx'
Shows the total size of the /var/log directory

days.setup_script:
# Create some dummy files for exercises
mkdir -p ~/test_dir/sub_dir
echo 'line1\nline2 error\nline3' > ~/test_dir/file1.txt
echo 'lineA\nlineB warn\nlineC' > ~/test_dir/sub_dir/file2.log
echo 'another log' > ~/test_dir/file3.log
tasks: Practice using `find` to locate specific files by name, type, and size.
Use `grep` with regular expressions to search for patterns in log files.
Monitor system processes and understand how to terminate them safely.

days.title: Linux Permissions, Users & Basic Networking
days.explanation: Understanding user and file permissions is vital for security and access control in server environments. Basic networking commands help diagnose connectivity issues and manage network interfaces, essential for distributed systems.

commands.desc: Sets read/write/execute for owner, read/execute for group and others
Creates a new user with a home directory
Displays network interface information
Sends an HTTP HEAD request to google.com

days.setup_script:
# Create a dummy file and directory for permission exercises
touch ~/test_file.txt
mkdir ~/test_dir_perms
# Ensure you have sudo access for user management if performing locally
tasks: Experiment with `chmod` and `chown` to change file and directory permissions.
Practice creating a new user and assigning them to a group.
Use `ping`, `ip`, and `curl` to check network connectivity and IP configuration.

days.title: Git Advanced Concepts & Branching Strategies
days.explanation: Git is the backbone of modern software development and DevOps. Beyond basic commits, understanding advanced branching, merging, and collaboration workflows is crucial for managing code in teams and CI/CD pipelines.

commands.desc: Creates and switches to a new branch
Merges the 'main' branch into the current branch
Rebases the current branch onto 'main'
Temporarily saves uncommitted changes

days.setup_script:
git init my_repo
cd my_repo
echo 'initial content' > file1.txt
git add .
git commit -m 'Initial commit'
tasks: Create a new branch, make changes, commit, and then merge it back to `main`.
Experiment with `git stash` to temporarily save and reapply changes.
Practice resolving a simple merge conflict.

phases.title: Phase 2: Docker - Containerization Fundamentals
phases.description: This phase introduces Docker, the leading containerization platform. You'll learn to build, run, and manage containers, understanding the core concepts vital for modern application deployment.

days.title: Docker Fundamentals: Images & Containers
days.explanation: Docker revolutionizes how applications are packaged and deployed by encapsulating them into isolated containers. Understanding images and containers is the first step to leveraging this powerful technology for consistent environments.
topics: Running and stopping containers (`docker run`, `docker ps`, `docker stop`)

commands.desc: Runs an Nginx container in detached mode, mapping port 80
Lists currently running containers
Stops a running container
Pulls the latest Ubuntu image

days.setup_script: # Docker is typically pre-installed on Killercoda scenarios
tasks: Run a simple Nginx container and access it from your browser (if using a local setup).
Explore various `docker run` options, including detached mode and port mapping.
Inspect container logs (`docker logs`) and processes (`docker top`).

days.title: Dockerfile & Building Custom Images
days.explanation: Dockerfiles are blueprints for creating custom Docker images, enabling consistent and reproducible builds of your applications. Mastering Dockerfile syntax is crucial for packaging your own software effectively.

commands.desc: Builds an image from the Dockerfile in the current directory
Runs a container from your custom image
Shows the layers of an image

days.setup_script:
mkdir my_docker_app
cd my_docker_app
echo 'FROM alpine:latest' > Dockerfile
echo 'CMD echo "Hello from Docker!"' >> Dockerfile
tasks: Create a simple Dockerfile to package a Python 'Hello World' script.
Build your custom image and run a container from it.
Experiment with different base images (e.g., `alpine`, `ubuntu`).

days.title: Docker Networking & Volumes
days.explanation: Containers need to communicate with each other and persist data. Docker networking allows containers to form isolated networks, while volumes provide a way to store and share data beyond the container's lifecycle, ensuring data integrity.

commands.desc: Creates a new bridge network
Runs Nginx in the 'my-net' network
Creates a named Docker volume
Runs a container, mounting a volume, and writes data to it

days.setup_script: # No specific setup needed, will create resources on the fly
tasks: Create a custom Docker network and run two containers in it, making them communicate.
Create a named Docker volume, write data into it from a container, and verify persistence.
Understand the difference between bind mounts and Docker volumes and when to use each.

days.title: Docker Compose - Multi-Container Applications
days.explanation: Real-world applications often consist of multiple interconnected services (e.g., web server, database). Docker Compose allows you to define and run multi-container Docker applications with a single YAML file and a single command, simplifying complex setups.
topics: Running and stopping multi-container apps (`docker-compose up`, `docker-compose down`)
Environment variables and secrets in Compose

commands.desc: Starts all services defined in docker-compose.yml in detached mode
Lists services run by Docker Compose
Stops and removes containers, networks, and volumes defined in Compose file
Executes a command inside a running service container

days.setup_script:
mkdir my_compose_app
cd my_compose_app
echo 'version: "3.8"' > docker-compose.yml
echo 'services:' >> docker-compose.yml
echo '  web:' >> docker-compose.yml
echo '    image: nginx' >> docker-compose.yml
echo '    ports:' >> docker-compose.yml
echo '      - "80:80"' >> docker-compose.yml
tasks: Create a `docker-compose.yml` file to run a web service (Nginx) and a simple database (e.g., Redis).
Start, stop, and restart the multi-container application using `docker-compose` commands.
Understand how services can communicate within a Compose setup.

phases.title: Phase 3: Kubernetes - Container Orchestration
phases.description: This phase delves into Kubernetes, the industry standard for orchestrating containerized applications. You'll learn how to deploy, scale, and manage applications across clusters, moving beyond single-machine Docker deployments.

days.title: Kubernetes Introduction & Minikube Setup
days.explanation: Kubernetes automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. Starting with Minikube provides a local single-node Kubernetes cluster for hands-on learning without needing a full cloud setup.
topics: What is Kubernetes? Why do we need it?
Kubernetes architecture (Master/Worker Nodes, Pods, Services, Deployments)

commands.desc: Starts the Minikube local Kubernetes cluster
Lists the nodes in the cluster
Displays information about the cluster
Checks the status of the Minikube cluster

days.setup_script: # Minikube and kubectl are usually pre-installed in Killercoda Kubernetes scenarios.
tasks: Start your Minikube cluster and verify its status.
Use `kubectl` to interact with the cluster and view basic information.
Understand the roles of master and worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster.

days.title: Pods & Deployments
days.explanation: Pods are the smallest deployable units in Kubernetes, encapsulating one or more containers. Deployments manage the desired state of Pods, enabling declarative updates and ensuring high availability for your applications.
topics: Deployments: creating, updating, and rolling back

commands.desc: Creates/updates resources defined in a YAML file
Lists all pods in the current namespace
Shows detailed information about a specific pod
Fetches logs for a pod

days.setup_script:
cat <<EOF > my-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx-deployment
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  replicas: 2
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
EOF
tasks: Create a YAML manifest for a simple Nginx deployment with 2 replicas.
Deploy the application using `kubectl apply` and verify the pods are running.
Practice scaling the deployment up and down using `kubectl scale`.

days.title: Services & Basic Ingress (Exposing Applications)
days.explanation: Services provide stable network endpoints for your Pods, abstracting away their dynamic nature. Ingress manages external access to services within a cluster, allowing HTTP/S routing from outside the cluster to internal services.
topics: Basic Ingress controller and Ingress resource

commands.desc: Creates a NodePort service for the Nginx deployment
Lists all services
Gets the URL to access the service via Minikube

days.setup_script:
cat <<EOF > my-service.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-nginx-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: nginx
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 80
  type: NodePort # Or ClusterIP, LoadBalancer
EOF
kubectl apply -f my-deployment.yaml # Assuming deployment from Day 9 is present
tasks: Create a `NodePort` service for your Nginx deployment.
Access the Nginx application using `minikube service`.
Understand when to use `ClusterIP`, `NodePort`, and `LoadBalancer` service types.

days.title: ConfigMaps & Secrets (Configuration Management)
days.explanation: ConfigMaps and Secrets are Kubernetes objects that allow you to decouple configuration data and sensitive information from your application code. This improves portability, security, and maintainability of your deployments.
topics: Secrets for sensitive data (e.g., passwords, API keys)
Mounting ConfigMaps and Secrets as files or environment variables

commands.desc: Creates a ConfigMap from a literal value
Creates a generic Secret from a literal value
Lists ConfigMaps
Describes a secret (value is base64 encoded)

days.setup_script:
cat <<EOF > pod-with-config.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: config-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: my-container
      image: busybox
      command: ["sh", "-c", "echo \$MY_ENV_VAR && sleep 3600"]
      env:
        - name: MY_ENV_VAR
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: my-config
              key: config_key
EOF
tasks: Create a ConfigMap and inject its data into a Pod as an environment variable.
Create a Secret and mount it as a file inside a Pod.
Understand why Secrets are base64 encoded, but not truly encrypted at rest by default.

phases.title: Phase 4: Automation & CI/CD Fundamentals
phases.description: This phase introduces fundamental concepts of automation using Ansible and the basics of Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) with a focus on GitHub Actions, essential for modern DevOps practices.

days.title: Introduction to Ansible (Basic Playbook)
days.explanation: Ansible is an open-source automation tool used for configuration management, application deployment, and task automation. Learning its declarative language allows for efficient and reproducible infrastructure management.
topics: Ansible architecture (Control Node, Managed Nodes, Inventory)

commands.desc: Pings all hosts in the inventory to check connectivity
Executes an Ansible playbook

days.setup_script:
echo '[local]' > inventory.ini
echo 'localhost ansible_connection=local' >> inventory.ini

cat <<EOF > my_playbook.yaml
- name: Install Nginx on localhost
  hosts: local
  become: yes
  tasks:
    - name: Update apt cache
      apt: update_cache=yes
    - name: Install Nginx
      apt: name=nginx state=present
    - name: Start Nginx service
      service: name=nginx state=started
EOF
tasks: Set up a basic Ansible inventory targeting your local machine.
Write a playbook to install a package (e.g., `nginx`) on your local machine.
Run the playbook and verify the installation.

days.title: CI/CD Principles & GitHub Actions Intro
days.explanation: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery) is a methodology that automates software delivery. GitHub Actions provides a flexible and powerful way to build, test, and deploy code directly from your GitHub repository, making it an excellent starting point for CI/CD.

commands.desc: Triggers GitHub Actions workflow after committing and pushing changes

days.setup_script:
mkdir -p .github/workflows
cat <<EOF > .github/workflows/hello-world.yaml
name: Hello World CI

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Run a one-line script
        run: echo "Hello, GitHub Actions!"
      - name: Run a multi-line script
        run |
          echo Add other actions to build, test, and deploy your project.
          echo For example, you can use the setup-node action to set up a Node.js environment.
EOF
tasks: Create a new GitHub repository.
Set up a basic GitHub Actions workflow that runs on push to `main` and simply prints a message.
Push changes and observe the workflow execution in the GitHub Actions tab.

days.title: Basic Scripting for DevOps (Shell/Python)
days.explanation: Scripting is indispensable for automating repetitive tasks, gluing together tools, and creating custom solutions in a DevOps environment. Basic proficiency in shell scripting and Python allows for powerful automation.
topics: Shell scripting basics (`bash`): variables, conditionals, loops
Python scripting for automation: `os` module, `subprocess` module

commands.desc: Executes a shell script
Executes a Python script

days.setup_script:
cat <<EOF > myscript.sh
#!/bin/bash
NAME="World"
if [ -n "$1" ]; then
  NAME="$1"
fi
echo "Hello, $NAME from Bash!"
EOF
chmod +x myscript.sh

cat <<EOF > my_script.py
import sys

def greet(name="World"):
    print(f"Hello, {name} from Python!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        greet(sys.argv[1])
    else:
        greet()
EOF
tasks: Write a shell script that takes an argument and prints a personalized greeting.
Write a Python script to perform a simple system command (e.g., list files in a directory).
Consider how these scripts could be integrated into a GitHub Actions workflow.
